Zimbabwe

Falls · Wild · Resilient

Key facts

CapitalHarare
Population15.1M
Language16 official languages
National sportFootball / Cricket
National dishSadza

About Zimbabwe

Zimbabwe is a country. Its capital city is Harare, with other major cities including Bulawayo and Mutare. With a population of approximately 15.1M, the main language spoken is 16 official languages.

Did you know

Victoria Falls produces a spray cloud visible from 50 km away — locals call it 'Mosi-oa-Tunya': The Smoke That Thunders.

Explore Zimbabwe

🏛

Capital

Harare serves as the political, cultural and economic heart of Zimbabwe. As the seat of government and often the most populous city, it concentrates the country's main institutions, universities and cultural landmarks. Beyond the capital, major cities include Bulawayo, Mutare — each a hub of regional culture, economy and history. Harare was founded as Fort Salisbury in 1890 by the British South Africa Company's Pioneer Column and was renamed at independence in 1980 after a Shona chief — Neharawa, meaning 'he who does not sleep' — and the National Gallery of Zimbabwe, opened in 1957, houses one of the continent's most significant collections of stone sculpture from the Shona school.

👥

People

With a population of approximately 15.1M, Zimbabwe is a vibrant society with a rich mix of traditions and communities. The official language is 16 official languages, which reflects the country's cultural heritage and connects it with a wide international community. Zimbabwe's stone-carving tradition — Shona sculpture — emerged in the 1950s when artist Frank McEwen encouraged local artists to work with the country's abundant verdite and springstone, producing a school of modernist figurative sculpture that reached major international galleries in London and New York within a decade.

🍽

Food

The emblematic dish of Zimbabwe is Sadza. Food culture in Zimbabwe is deeply tied to local identity — shared meals and markets are central to daily life and social gatherings across the country.

🏅

Sport

Football / Cricket holds a special place in the heart of Zimbabwe's national identity. Zimbabwe's cricket team produced Henry Olonga and Andy Flower, who wore black armbands at the 2003 World Cup to mourn 'the death of democracy in Zimbabwe' — one of sport's most deliberate political statements, delivered at the risk of prosecution and later exile, by players who prioritised conscience over career.

⛰

Nature

The highest point in Zimbabwe is Mount Nyangani, rising to 2,592 metres above sea level. Victoria Falls — Mosi-oa-Tunya, 'the smoke that thunders' in Tonga — spans 1,708 metres across the Zambezi River gorge and produces a spray cloud visible 50 kilometres away; David Livingstone became the first European to see it in 1855 and described it as a sight 'so lovely it must have been gazed upon by angels'.

Major cities

Harare Capital
Bulawayo
Mutare
